code:
export const blogPosts = [{
  id: 1,
  slug: "fitness-discipline-saves-money",
  title: "How Fitness Discipline Helps You Save Money",
  category: "Finance & Fitness",
  categoryColor: "orange",
  date: "Jan 15, 2026",
  readTime: "6 min read",
  excerpt: "The same mental habits that push you through a tough workout — delayed gratification, consistency, goal-setting — are exactly what make a great saver.",
  image: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1758966071975-b58c681f05e0?w=800&q=80",
  content: `When you commit to waking up at 6 AM for a run, you're building more than just cardiovascular endurance — you're training your brain to choose long-term rewards over short-term comfort. That's the exact mindset that separates financially successful people from those who live paycheck to paycheck.

**Delayed Gratification is a Muscle**

Athletes know that results don't come overnight. A marathon runner doesn't expect to finish 26.2 miles without months of training. Similarly, building wealth requires patience. Skipping that daily latte isn't about deprivation — it's about training the muscle of delayed gratification.

**Setting Fitness Goals = Setting Financial Goals**

Every serious athlete tracks their performance. Miles logged, PRs hit, calories burned. The same principle applies to money. When you start budgeting with the same intensity you track your macros, your savings rate climbs.

**Community Accountability**

Join a running club and you'll rarely skip a morning jog. The same is true of financial accountability partners. Tell someone your savings goal and watch how your habits shift.`,
  tags: ["discipline", "saving", "mindset"]
}, {
  id: 2,
  slug: "50-30-20-budget-rule",
  title: "The 50/30/20 Budget Rule: A Runner's Approach",
  category: "Finance",
  categoryColor: "emerald",
  date: "Jan 22, 2026",
  readTime: "5 min read",
  excerpt: "Think of your paycheck as a training plan. 50% for needs (recovery), 30% for wants (active days), 20% for savings (race day investment).",
  image: "https://images.pexels.com/photos/259091/pexels-photo-259091.jpeg?w=800&q=80",
  content: `The 50/30/20 rule is one of the simplest and most effective budgeting frameworks — and it maps perfectly to a runner's training philosophy.

**50% — Needs (Your Recovery Days)**

These are non-negotiables: rent, groceries, utilities, insurance. Just like rest days are non-negotiable for injury prevention, your essential expenses must be protected first.

**30% — Wants (Your Active Training Days)**

Dining out, streaming services, gym upgrades, weekend adventures. These are valuable but flexible. On hard training weeks, you cut the extras. When life is good, you enjoy the rewards.

**20% — Savings & Investments (Your Race Day Fund)**

This is where the magic happens. Emergency fund, retirement accounts, index funds. Think of it as your race day performance — everything you've been building toward.

**How to Start**

1. List all monthly income
2. Calculate 50% — that's your needs ceiling
3. Whatever's left: split 60/40 between wants and savings
4. Automate the savings transfer on payday`,
  tags: ["budgeting", "50/30/20", "personal finance"]
}, {
  id: 3,
  slug: "meal-prep-body-wallet",
  title: "Meal Prep: Good for Your Body AND Your Wallet",
  category: "Wellness & Finance",
  categoryColor: "blue",
  date: "Feb 1, 2026",
  readTime: "4 min read",
  excerpt: "Cooking in bulk on Sundays doesn't just fuel your workouts — it can save you $300-500 a month compared to eating out daily.",
  image: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1770761070158-33f939b42799?w=800&q=80",
  content: `The average American spends $3,000+ per year eating out. A serious meal prepper spends a fraction of that while eating cleaner, more nutritious food. It's the ultimate dual win.

**The Numbers Don't Lie**

- Average restaurant meal: $15-20
- Home-cooked equivalent: $3-5
- 5 lunches per week, 50 weeks: saves up to $3,750 annually

**What to Prep for Peak Performance**

Focus on protein, complex carbs, and healthy fats:
- Grilled chicken breasts (batch cook 8-10 at once)
- Brown rice or quinoa (rice cooker makes this effortless)
- Roasted vegetables (one sheet pan, four servings)
- Overnight oats for pre-workout breakfasts

**Getting Started**

Sunday is your power hour. Two hours of prep equals five days of clean, affordable eating. Invest in quality glass containers — they last years and pay for themselves within a month of meal prepping.`,
  tags: ["meal prep", "nutrition", "saving money"]
}, {
  id: 4,
  slug: "gym-habits-financial-habits",
  title: "5 Ways Gym Habits Build Financial Habits",
  category: "Finance & Fitness",
  categoryColor: "orange",
  date: "Feb 8, 2026",
  readTime: "7 min read",
  excerpt: "Tracking reps, showing up consistently, and progressive overload — these gym principles translate directly into wealth-building strategies.",
  image: "https://images.pexels.com/photos/36717053/pexels-photo-36717053.jpeg?w=800&q=80",
  content: `Serious gym-goers understand something most people don't: transformation happens through boring, consistent, incremental effort. That's also the secret to financial success.

**1. Track Everything**

In the gym: log every set, rep, and weight. In finance: track every dollar in and out. What gets measured gets managed.

**2. Progressive Overload = Consistent Investment**

Adding 2.5 lbs to your squat each week seems tiny — until you look back six months later. Adding $50 to your monthly investment contribution has the same compounding effect.

**3. Rest Days Prevent Burnout**

Hustle culture glorifies the grind 24/7. But overtraining leads to injury. Financially, this means avoiding burnout by celebrating small wins and not obsessing over daily market fluctuations.

**4. Find Your Form Before Going Heavy**

Master the basics before loading the bar. Financially: build your emergency fund before investing in individual stocks or crypto.

**5. Community Keeps You Accountable**

The gym buddy effect is real. So is a financial accountability partner or community like Sweat & Save.`,
  tags: ["habits", "discipline", "investing"]
}, {
  id: 5,
  slug: "beginner-investing-guide",
  title: "Beginner's Guide to Investing: Start With $50",
  category: "Finance",
  categoryColor: "emerald",
  date: "Feb 14, 2026",
  readTime: "8 min read",
  excerpt: "You don't need thousands to start investing. Here's how to build a solid portfolio starting with just $50 a month.",
  image: "https://images.pexels.com/photos/259091/pexels-photo-259091.jpeg?w=800&q=80",
  content: `The biggest investing myth is that you need a lot of money to get started. The truth: consistency beats size every single time, thanks to compound interest.

**Step 1: Build Your Emergency Fund First**

Before you invest a single dollar, have 3-6 months of expenses in a high-yield savings account. This is your financial foundation — your base fitness level.

**Step 2: Take Your Employer 401(k) Match**

If your employer matches contributions, take the full match. It's an instant 50-100% return on your money — no investment beats that.

**Step 3: Open a Roth IRA**

For most beginners, a Roth IRA (tax-free growth) is the best vehicle. You can open one at Fidelity, Vanguard, or Schwab with no minimum.

**Step 4: Invest in Index Funds**

Don't pick stocks — buy the whole market. A Total Market Index Fund (like VTSAX) gives you exposure to thousands of companies for low fees.

**Step 5: Automate and Forget**

Set up automatic monthly contributions. Then stop looking at it daily. Time in the market beats timing the market.`,
  tags: ["investing", "index funds", "beginner finance"]
}, {
  id: 6,
  slug: "mental-health-fitness-wealth",
  title: "Mental Health: The Missing Link Between Fitness and Wealth",
  category: "Wellness",
  categoryColor: "blue",
  date: "Feb 20, 2026",
  readTime: "6 min read",
  excerpt: "Stress is the #1 enemy of both athletic performance and financial decision-making. Here's how to protect your mental game.",
  image: "https://images.pexels.com/photos/20528492/pexels-photo-20528492.jpeg?w=800&q=80",
  content: `Research shows that chronic stress impairs both physical performance and financial decision-making. When you're overwhelmed, you skip workouts AND make impulsive purchases. Managing your mental health isn't a luxury — it's a performance multiplier.

**The Stress-Spending Cycle**

Stressed out? Many people turn to retail therapy or unhealthy food. Both drain your bank account and your health simultaneously. Breaking this cycle is one of the highest-leverage moves you can make.

**Exercise as a Mental Reset**

Cardio releases BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor) — literally fertilizer for your brain. Even a 20-minute walk can shift your mental state enough to make better financial decisions.

**Mindfulness for Better Money Decisions**

Before any non-essential purchase over $50, implement a 24-hour rule. This pause mimics the mindful approach athletes use before making training decisions: Is this helping or hurting my goals?

**Sleep: The Ultimate Performance Enhancer**

Sleep-deprived people make worse financial decisions (studies confirm this). Prioritize 7-9 hours. It's the cheapest, most effective performance supplement available.`,
  tags: ["mental health", "stress", "wellness", "money mindset"]
}];
export const fitnessWorkouts = [{
  id: 1,

  duration: "30 min",

  exercises: ["20s Burpees", "10s Rest", "20s Mountain Climbers", "10s Rest", "20s Jump Squats"],
  accent: "orange"
}, {
  id: 2,
  title: "5K Training Plan",
  duration: "8 weeks",

  calories: "250-300 kcal/session",

  exercises: ["Week 1: 1min run / 2min walk x8", "Week 4: 3min run / 1min walk x6", "Week 8: 30min continuous run"],
  accent: "orange"
}, {
  id: 3,
  title: "Strength Builder",
  duration: "45 min",
  level: "All Levels",
  calories: "200-280 kcal",

}, {

  accent: "orange"
}];

  accent: "emerald"
}, {
  id: 2,
  title: "Automate Your Savings",
  category: "Saving",
  amount: "Pay yourself first",
  description: "Set up an automatic transfer to savings on payday. You can't spend what you never see in your checking account.",
  accent: "emerald"
}, {
  id: 3,
  title: "The 24-Hour Rule",
  category: "Budgeting",
  amount: "Avoid impulse buys",
  description: "Wait 24 hours before any non-essential purchase over $30. Most urges disappear — and so does the regret.",
  accent: "emerald"
}, {

  title: "Negotiate Everything",
  category: "Hacks",
  amount: "Save $200-500/mo",
  description: "Call your internet, insurance, and phone providers annually. Ask for a better rate. It works more often than you think.",
